# Efficiency and consistency enhancement for alkaline electrolyzers driven by renewable energy sources

[*Communications Engineering*](/commseng)
**volume 2**, Article number: 22 (2023)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

20k Accesses

45 Citations

5 Altmetric

[Metrics details](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7/metrics)

### Subjects

## Abstract

Low-cost alkaline water electrolysis from renewable energy sources (RESs) is suitable for large-scale hydrogen production. However, fluctuating RESs lead to poor performance of alkaline water electrolyzers (AWEs) at low loads. Here we explore two urgent performance issues: inefficiency and inconsistency. Through detailed operation process analysis of AWEs and the established equivalent electrical model, we reveal the mechanisms of inefficiency and inconsistency of low-load AWEs are related to the physical structure and electrical characteristics. Furthermore, we propose a multi-mode self-optimization electrolysis converting strategy to improve the efficiency and consistency of AWEs. In particular, compared to a conventional dc power supply, we demonstrate using a lab-scale and large-scale commercially available AWE that the maximum efficiency can be doubled while the operation range of the electrolyzer can be extended from 30–100% to 10–100% of rated load. Our method can be easily generalized and can facilitate hydrogen production from RESs.

## Introduction

Nowadays, hydrogen has gotten great attention because of the pronounced environmental and climate problems caused by carbon-intensive fossil energies[1](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#ref-CR1 "Glenk, G. & Reichelstein, S. Economics of converting renewable power to hydrogen. Nat. Energy 4, 216–222 (2019)."),[2](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#ref-CR2 "Lenz, O. Hydrogen comes alive. Nat. Energy 5, 426–427 (2020)."). With its clean, versatile, and lightweight properties, hydrogen is considered the most promising solution that can help reduce the carbon emissions of transportation[3](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#ref-CR3 "Cano, Z. P. et al. Batteries and fuel cells for emerging electric vehicle markets. Nat. Energy 3, 279–289 (2018)."), metallurgy[4](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#ref-CR4 "Wang, R. R. et al. Hydrogen direct reduction (H-DR) in steel industry—an overview of challenges and opportunities. J. Clean. Prod. 329, 129797–129807 (2021)."), chemical industry[5](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#ref-CR5 "Ma, Y. et al. Hydrogen and ethanol: production, storage, and transportation. Int. J. Hydrog. Energy 46, 27330–27348 (2021)."), and other sectors[6](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#ref-CR6 "Daehn, K. et al. Innovations to decarbonize materials industries. Nat. Rev. Mater. 7, 275–294 (2022)."). As a result, the hydrogen demand has been growing exponentially, reaching 70 million tons in 2018 and is expected to reach 545 million tons per year in 2050[7](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#ref-CR7 "IEA. The Future of Hydrogen—Analysis 
                  https://www.iea.org/reports/the-future-of-hydrogen
                  
                 (2019)."). However, most of the world’s hydrogen is currently obtained by the reformation of fossil energies, which consumes much energy and causes global CO2 emissions to reach more than 830 million tonnes per year[7](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#ref-CR7 "IEA. The Future of Hydrogen—Analysis 
                  https://www.iea.org/reports/the-future-of-hydrogen
                  
                 (2019)."). For sustainable development, hydrogen production must be efficient and environmentally friendly. Therefore, hydrogen production technology by electrolyzing water using surplus photovoltaic power, wind power, and other renewable electricity, namely green hydrogen, has become a hot research topic[8](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#ref-CR8 "Song, S. et al. Production of hydrogen from offshore wind in China and cost-competitive supply to Japan. Nat. Commun. 12, 6953–6960 (2021)."),[9](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#ref-CR9 "Kakoulaki, G. et al. Green hydrogen in Europe–a regional assessment: substituting existing production with electrolysis powered by renewables. Energy Convers. Manag. 228, 113649–113667 (2021).").

Currently, there are three electrolytic hydrogen methods, solid oxide electrolyzers (SOEs), proton exchange membrane (PEM) electrolyzers, and AWEs. SOEs constitute an advanced concept enabling water or steam electrolysis at high temperatures (600–900 °C)[10](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#ref-CR10 "Lang, M. et al. Long term behavior of solid oxide electrolyser (SOEC) stacks. ECS. Trans. 91, 2713–2725 (2019)."), whose efficiency is higher than PEM electrolyzers and AWEs. As for the practical application, SOEs meet remarkable challenges as concerns the thermal stability of materials, gas mixture, and sealing issues. Hence, SOEs are still at the R&D stage. Compared to SOEs, PEM electrolyzers and AWEs are commercially available. PEM electrolyzers are more efficient and allow for higher current densities than AWEs. One obvious disadvantage of PEM electrolyzers is the high capital cost of their acid-tolerance components like the Nafion membrane, titanium bipolar plates, and novel metal catalysts PT/C and IrO2[11](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#ref-CR11 "Yuan, W., Zhao, M., Yuan, J. & Li, C. M. Ni foam supported three-dimensional vertically aligned and networked layered CoO nanosheet/ graphene hybrid array as a high-performance oxygen evolution electrode. J. Power Sources 319, 159–167 (2016)."). In addition, their shorter lifetimes than AWEs have also hindered their application in large-scale power-to-gas scenarios[12](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#ref-CR12 "Partidário, P., Aguiar, R., Martins, P., Rangel, C. M. & Cabrita, I. The hydrogen roadmap in the Portuguese energy system–developing the P2G case. Int. J. Hydrog. Energy 45, 25646–25657 (2020)."). In contrast, AWEs is a relatively mature technology that has been developed over 100 years. For commercial AWEs, earth-abundant electrocatalysts are stable enough to run both half-reactions, whose lifetime can reach up to 15 years. Hence, AWEs are very suitable for large-scale electrolytic hydrogen projects[13](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#ref-CR13 "Ivy, J. Summary of Electrolytic Hydrogen Production. Milestone Completion Report No. NREL/MP-560-36734 (National Renewable Energy Lab, 2004)."),[14](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#ref-CR14 "Ursua, A., Gandia, L. M. & Sanchis, P. Hydrogen production from water electrolysis: current status and future trends. IEEE 100, 410–426 (2011).").

Although projects with AWEs up to 6 MW exist in practice[15](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#ref-CR15 "Bailera, M., Lisbona, P., Romeo, L. M. & Espatolero, S. Power to Gas projects review: lab, pilot and demo plants for storing renewable energy and CO2. Renew. Sust. Energy Rev. 69, 292–312 (2017)."), the operational flexibility of AWEs still needs to be improved, especially when they are powered by wide-range fluctuant RESs. One widely concerned challenge is the impurity problem that the low-load AWEs (usually 25–45% of rated load) could potentially lead to gas crossover between the cathode and anode. This impurity will result in the formation of flammable gas mixture[16](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#ref-CR16 "Janssen, H., Bringmann, J. C., Emonts, B. & Schröder, V. Safety-related studies on hydrogen production in high-pressure electrolysers. Int. J. Hydrog. Energy 29, 759–770 (2004)."), especially for the anode where 2 vol% H2 in O2 corresponds to about 50% of the lower explosive limit. Therefore, when the supplied RESs, like photovoltaic power, frequently fluctuate in a wide range, the start-stops of AWEs are increased obviously to ensure the system’s safety. These frequent start-stops have a great influence on the stability and power quality of the electrical power system[17](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#ref-CR17 "Liu, S., Liu, P. X. & Wang, X. Stability analysis of grid-interfacing inverter control in distribution systems with multiple photovoltaic-based distributed generators. IEEE Trans. Ind. Electron. 63, 7339–7348 (2016)."),[18](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#ref-CR18 "Meng, X., Chen, M., He, M., Wang, X. & Liu, J. A novel high power hybrid rectifier with low cost and high grid current quality for improved efficiency of electrolytic hydrogen production. IEEE Trans. Power Electron. 37, 3763–3768 (2021)."); at the same time, the RESs cannot be fully consumed because of the low-load curtailment of AWEs. In addition, the long-term shutdown will cause reverse currents for the AWEs[19](#ref-CR19 "Haleem, A. A. et al. Effects of operation and shutdown parameters and electrode materials on the reverse current phenomenon in alkaline water analyzers. J. Power Sources 535, 231454–231465 (2022)."),[20](#ref-CR20 "Haleem, A. A. et al. A new accelerated durability test protocol for water oxidation electrocatalysts of renewable energy powered alkaline water electrolyzers. Electrochemistry 89, 186–191 (2021)."),[21](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#ref-CR21 "Uchino, Y. et al. Relationship between the redox reactions on a bipolar plate and reverse current after alkaline water electrolysis. Electrochemistry 9, 67–74 (2018)."), which will adversely affect the durability of electrodes. Steady and dynamic models of gas impurity caused by gas crossover are established, considering several influence factors. To sum up, the gas impurity problem is caused by two reasons: the crossover through the diaphragm by gas diffusion[22](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#ref-CR22 "Schalenbach, M., Lueke, W. & Stolten, D. Hydrogen diffusivity and electrolyte permeability of the Zirfon PERL separator for alkaline water electrolysis. J. Electrochem. Soc. 163, F1480 (2016)."),[23](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#ref-CR23 "Hodges, A. et al. A high-performance capillary-fed electrolysis cell promises more cost-competitive renewable hydrogen. Nat. Commun. 13, 1304 (2022).") and the crossover by the circulated electrolyte mixing[24](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#ref-CR24 "Haug, P., Koj, M. & Turek, T. Influence of process conditions on gas purity in alkaline water electrolysis. Int. J. Hydrog. Energy 42, 9406–9418 (2017)."),[25](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#ref-CR25 "David, M., Ocampo-Martínez, C. & Sánchez-Peña, R. Advances in alkaline water electrolyzers: a review. J. Energy Storage 23, 392–403 (2019)."). To enhance the gas purity for low-load AWEs, several strategies are also proposed. Anion exchange membrane[26](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#ref-CR26 "Li, D. et al. Highly quaternized polystyrene ionomers for high performance anion exchange membrane water electrolysers. Nat. Energy 5, 378–385 (2020)."),[27](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#ref-CR27 "Hickner, M. A., Herring, A. M. & Coughlin, E. B. Anion exchange membranes: current status and moving forward. J. Polym. Sci. B Polym. Phys. 51, 1727–1735 (2013).") and other novel diaphragm structures are developed to prevent the crossover through the diaphragm by gas diffusion. Based on the same goal, Qi et al. propose a pressure control strategy to extend the load range of AWEs[28](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#ref-CR28 "Qi, R. et al. Pressure control strategy to extend the loading range of an alkaline electrolysis system. Int. J. Hydrog. Energy 46, 35997–36011 (2021)."). On the other hand, Schug regulates the electrolyte circulation rate adaptively to reduce the crossover by the circulated electrolyte mixing[29](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#ref-CR29 "Schug, C. A. Operational characteristics of high-pressure, high-efficiency water-hydrogen-electrolysis. Int. J. Hydrog. Energy 23, 1113–1120 (1998)."). In order to solve the gas impurity problem completely, a novel alkaline electrolysis system is designed[30](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#ref-CR30 "Chen, L., Dong, X., Wang, Y. & Xia, Y. Separating hydrogen and oxygen evolution in alkaline water electrolysis using nickel hydroxide. Nat. Commun. 7, 11741 (2016)."), which separates hydrogen and oxygen evolution. But its reliability needs to be further verified.

Through the above strategies, the impurity problem of low-load AWEs has been alleviated. However, in this work, we find that the inefficiency and inconsistency problems still hinder the operation of low-load AWEs, except for the impurity problem. For the low-load AWEs, the efficiency of electrolyzers is very low, and the operation states of different cells of electrolyzers are not consistent. The inefficiency problem will cause extra energy dissipation, and the inconsistency problem will cause obvious lifetime degradation of long-running cells. Because corresponding mechanisms are not analyzed effectively, some conventional methods, like novel catalysts[31](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#ref-CR31 "Zhou, K. L. et al. Platinum single-atom catalyst coupled with transition metal/metal oxide heterostructure for accelerating alkaline hydrogen evolution reaction. Nat. Commun. 12, 3783 (2021)."), highly consistent cells of AWEs[32](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#ref-CR32 "Ren, P. et al. Novel analytic method of membrane electrode assembly parameters for fuel cell consistency evaluation by micro-current excitation. Appl. Energy 306, 118068 (2022)."), and so on, cannot well solve these two problems. Here, we analyze the detailed operation process of AWEs and establish the equivalent electrical model. Then, the inefficiency and inconsistency mechanisms of low-load AWEs are revealed. It is found that the physical structures and electrical characteristics but not the chemical properties of AWEs have a decisive influence on the low-load performance. Based on this, a multi-mode self-optimization electrolysis converting strategy is proposed to enhance the efficiency and consistency of AWEs. Its effectiveness is verified by a 2 Nm3/h commercial AWE, except for the lab-scale test system. Especially compared to the conventional dc power supply, the electrolyzer efficiency is increased from 29.27 to 53.21% under 15% of the rated load for the 2 Nm3/h commercial AWE. At the same time, under the condition that the system efficiency is larger than 50%, the operation range of the electrolyzer is extended from 30~100% to 10~100% of the rated load. The proposed method just changes the power supply and does not need to modify the components of electrolyzers; it can be easily generalized and can facilitate hydrogen production from RESs.

## Results

### Problem presentation

To illustrate the inefficiency and inconsistency phenomena of low-load AWEs, several experiments are conducted based on a 2 Nm3/h at 80 °C commercial AWE (electrical power is about 10 kW at 80 °C) and a simple AWE. The 2 Nm3/h commercial AWE is mainly used for practical verification, while the simple AWE is mainly designed for principal experiments due to the convenient observation and measurements.

As shown in Fig. [1a](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#Fig1), the cell is the basic element of the AWE; the cell consists of bipolar plates, anode/cathode catalysts, and a diaphragm. Usually, commercial AWEs are the bipolar form[14](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#ref-CR14 "Ursua, A., Gandia, L. M. & Sanchis, P. Hydrogen production from water electrolysis: current status and future trends. IEEE 100, 410–426 (2011)."),[25](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#ref-CR25 "David, M., Ocampo-Martínez, C. & Sánchez-Peña, R. Advances in alkaline water electrolyzers: a review. J. Energy Storage 23, 392–403 (2019)."),[29](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#ref-CR29 "Schug, C. A. Operational characteristics of high-pressure, high-efficiency water-hydrogen-electrolysis. Int. J. Hydrog. Energy 23, 1113–1120 (1998)."),[33](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#ref-CR33 "Ursúa, A. & Sanchis, P. Static–dynamic modelling of the electrical behaviour of a commercial advanced alkaline water electrolyser. Int. J. Hydrog. Energy 37, 18598–18614 (2012)."), composed of several cells connected in series (for the adopted AWE, the total cells are 48). Under this condition, each electrode, except for the first and the last ones, has two polarities, the anode side and the cathode side, which belong to two adjacent cells. In addition, the hydrogen channel, oxygen channel, and electrolyte channel are shared by all cells. For the adopted AWE, the electrolyte is an aqueous solution of KOH at 30 wt.% since its ionic conductivity is the highest at this concentration.

![figure 1](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs44172-023-00070-7/MediaObjects/44172_2023_70_Fig1_HTML.png)

These experiments can illustrate the inefficiency phenomenon. The whole AWE consists of 48 cells, and the maximum current density is 600 mA/cm2; the electrolyte is an aqueous solution of KOH at 30 wt.%. The whole system is supplied by the dc voltage source. The electrical power of the AWE is about 10 kW at 80 °C. **a** Principle structure of commercial AWEs (bipolar form). **b** Voltage-current curves under different temperatures; here, the voltage is the terminal voltage of the whole electrolyzer, and the current is the input current of the whole electrolyzer. The experiment was repeated five times. **c** Efficiency-power curves at 80 °C; here, the power is the input power of the whole electrolyzer, and efficiency = HHV of H2/ Electric energy consumption. Error bars in (**c**) denote the standard deviation where \(n=5\). The experiment was repeated five times. The source data for Fig. [1b,](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#Fig1)[c](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#Fig1) are available in Dataset 1.

When the AWE is powered by the dc voltage source from 0 to 100 V, the relationship between the total electrolytic voltage and electrolytic current is presented in Fig. [1b](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#Fig1). From the figure, the following observations should be noticed. (1) According to the series circuit, the theoretical reserve voltage of the whole electrolyzer is about 59 V (48\(\times\)1.23 V). However, under 59 V, the electrolytic current is still generated. (2) For the low-load range (<15 A) and high-load range (>15 A), there is an obvious linear relationship between the voltage and current, but with different slopes, the low-load equivalent resistance is much larger than the high-load equivalent resistance. That is, the system parameters or states are changed. (3) In the whole range, the relationship between the electrolytic voltage and electrolytic current does not meet the typical electrolysis hydrogen model presented in refs. [14](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#ref-CR14 "Ursua, A., Gandia, L. M. & Sanchis, P. Hydrogen production from water electrolysis: current status and future trends. IEEE 100, 410–426 (2011)."),[33](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#ref-CR33 "Ursúa, A. & Sanchis, P. Static–dynamic modelling of the electrical behaviour of a commercial advanced alkaline water electrolyser. Int. J. Hydrog. Energy 37, 18598–18614 (2012)."),[34](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#ref-CR34 "Ulleberg, Ø. Modeling of advanced alkaline electrolyzers: a system simulation approach. Int. J. Hydrog. Energy 28, 21–33 (2003).").

Fig. [1c](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#Fig1) shows the system efficiency curves with the variation of input power. Here, efficiency is defined as

For the single cell, Eq. ([1](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#Equ1)) can be further expressed as

where \({\eta }\_{F}\) is the Faraday efficiency, \(F\) is the Faraday constant, \({{{{{{\rm{HHV}}}}}}}\_{{{{{{\rm{H}}}}}}2}\) is the higher heating value of 1 mol hydrogen, \({I}\_{{cell}}\) is the electrolytic current, and \({V}\_{{cell}}\) is the electrolytic voltage.

Based on Eq. ([2](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#Equ2)), if the electrolyzer is considered the pure series of multiple cells, the system efficiency is decreased as the input power is increased when it is supplied by the dc voltage source. However, From Fig. [1c](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#Fig1), it can be seen that the system efficiency changes non-monotonically with the variation of input power. The low-load efficiency is lower than the high-load efficiency, obviously. Especially the system efficiency is lower than 30% under 15% of the rated load. These experimental results illustrate the inefficiency phenomenon of low-load AWEs.

To further know the operation process of the AWE, the low-load experiments of a simple AWE are conducted for its convenient observation and measurements. As shown in Fig. [2a](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#Fig2), except for the electrode materials, the small-scale AWE has a similar structure to the large-scale commercial AWE.

![figure 2](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs44172-023-00070-7/MediaObjects/44172_2023_70_Fig2_HTML.png)

These experiments can illustrate the inconsistency phenomenon. The small-scale AWE simulates the large-scale commercial AWE. The bottom channel (the height is *b*) simulates the electrolyte channel, and there are four cells. Its bipolar plates are stainless steel; the electrolyte is an aqueous solution of KOH at 30 wt.%. The whole system is supplied by the dc voltage source. **a** Principle structure of the simple AWE, the size of the electrolyzer is 50 × 10 × 12 cm, the height of bottom channel *b* = 0.2 cm, the height of bipolar plates *h* = 6 cm, the size of bipolar plates is 10 × 10 cm and the width *w* = 0.1 cm, the distance between bipolar plates *d* = 12 cm. **b** Voltage-current curves at different temperatures; here the voltage is the terminal voltage of the whole electrolyzer, and the current is the input current of the whole electrolyzer. The experiment was repeated five times. The source data for Fig. [2b](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#Fig2) are available in Dataset 1.

The relationship between the total electrolytic voltage and electrolytic current is presented in Fig. [2b](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#Fig2), which is similar to Fig. [1b](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#Fig1). Fig. [3](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#Fig3) shows the operating states of different cells under the operating point 1 in Fig. [2b](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#Fig2), where the electrolytic voltage is 3 V and is less than the reserve voltage 4.92 V (4\(\times\)1.23 V). It can be seen that only the first and the last bipolar plates have obvious bubbles, and the middle plates do not have any bubbles. It can verify the existence of current when the electrolytic voltage is less than the reserve voltage. At the same time, it also means that the current only flows through plate1 and plate5 but does not flow through the middle plates. That is, electrolytic reactions occur only on the solid–liquid interfaces of plate1 and plate5 but do not occur on the solid–liquid interfaces of middle plates. Therefore, the operating states of different cells of low-load AWEs are not consistent, namely, the aforementioned inefficiency phenomenon.

![figure 3](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs44172-023-00070-7/MediaObjects/44172_2023_70_Fig3_HTML.png)

**a** 3 V < the reserve voltage 4.92 V. **b** 6 V > the reserve voltage 4.92 V. **c** 9 V > the reserve voltage 4.92 V. **d** 15 V > the reserve voltage 4.92 V.

Increasing the electrolytic voltage to 6 V, which is larger than the reserve voltage 4.92 V, namely the operating point 2 in Fig. [2b](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#Fig2), the operating states of different cells are shown in Fig. [3b](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#Fig3). However, it can be seen that there are still no reactions on the middle plates, while the reactions on plate1 and plate5 are stronger. Continuously increasing the electrolytic voltage to 9 V, namely the operating point 3 in Fig. [2b](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#Fig2), it can be seen that the upper parts of the middle plates have reactions, but the lower parts of the middle plates still have no reactions as shown in Fig. [3c](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#Fig3). The middle plates have obvious reactions as shown in Fig. [3d](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#Fig3) till the electrolytic voltage reaches to 15 V, namely the operating point 4 in Fig. [2b](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#Fig2), which is much larger than the reserve voltage. Hence, the electrolyzer cannot be viewed as a simple series of multiple cells, and its operation process is not just a generalization of the single cell; more is different[35](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#ref-CR35 "Anderson, P. W. More is different: broken symmetry and the nature of the hierarchical structure of science. Science 177, 393–396 (1972).").

### Mechanisms analysis

In this section, the mechanisms of the inefficiency and inconsistency of low-load AWEs are analyzed. Fig. [4a](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#Fig4) shows the electrical process of low-load AWEs. Due to the electrolyte channel, the connection of bipolar plates is not the pure series; any two of them are connected. Especially the first and the last bipolar plates are connected. Hence, when the terminal voltage of the electrolyzer \({U}\_{z}\) >the reserve voltage of one cell \({U}\_{o}\)(namely 1.23 V), the OER occurs on the solid–liquid interfaces of the positive electrode, and the HER occurs on the solid–liquid interfaces of the negative electrode. The hydroxide ions pass through the electrolyte channel and partial cell spaces, as shown in Fig. [4a](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#Fig4), then the electrolytic current is generated, which is called the start-up current in this paper. This can explain the experimental results shown in Fig. [3a](/articles/s44172-023-00070-7#Fig3).

![figure 4](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs44172-023-00070-7/MediaObjects/44172_2023_70_Fig4_HTML.png)

If the bipolar plate produces electrolytic reactions, its interfacial potential difference must be larger than the reserve voltage. Electrolytic reactions include Oxygen Evolution Reaction (OER) and Hydrogen Evolution Reaction (HER). For commercial AWEs, the electrolyte channel connects the first and the last bipolar plates; they can form a cell. Hence, the electrolytic current can be generated under the low terminal voltage. However, the interfacial potential differences of the middle plates are only generated by the voltage drop of this electrolytic current through the electrolyte channel. Context& scale
--------------

The authors of this perspective article present the industrial challenges of deploying alkaline electrolyzers for the large-scale production of renewable hydrogen. Alkaline electrolyzers are attractive for this purpose because of their simple design and low-cost materials. However, they also face new demands related to scaling up: reducing manufacturing costs, enhancing safety, operating at high pressure, increasing efficiency, and adapting to variable load. The authors point out that this technology has received less attention and improvement than other types of electrolyzers, such as proton exchange membrane or high-temperature electrolyzers, despite its long history of use.

To bridge this gap, the authors suggest some fundamental topics that could be investigated at different levels of the system, from components to stacks. They highlight the need to understand the behavior of catalysts in industrial conditions, especially in the presence of additives or impurities in the electrolyte. They also explore the role of water solvation and (nano)-bubbles in the reactivity and transport of water and gas in the system. Furthermore, they examine the impact of design and load on the lifetime of the electrolyzer and propose ways to better quantify it.

The authors emphasize that the first large plants using alkaline electrolyzers are expected to be operational by 2030 and that many more will follow. They also note that these plants will require the replacement or upgrade of their electrolyzer stacks in the future. Therefore, they urge the academic community to engage in these fundamental research topics that will lead to improved industrial products in the coming years and decades.

Summary
-------

For the sustainable development of the renewable hydrogen sector, [alkaline electrolysis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/alkaline-electrolysis "Learn more about alkaline electrolysis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") appears to be a technology of choice. Nevertheless, despite its long history that showed its reliability in the industrial field and despite the traditional belief from the scientific community, alkaline [electrolyzer technology](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/electrolyzer-technology "Learn more about electrolyzer technology from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") still requires optimization to meet the new challenges imposed by the deployment of large electrolysis plants, notably in the case of future massive islanded renewable [hydrogen production](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/hydrogen-production "Learn more about hydrogen production from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") plants. In fact, it turns out to be critical to reduce capital expenses (CAPEXs) costs, improve energy efficiency over time, and improve the dynamic response to [intermittent power](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/intermittent-power "Learn more about intermittent power from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") sources to lower operating expenses (OPEXs) costs while operating safely. In this context, some very fundamental challenges related to electrode degradation, electrolyte and [bubble transport](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/bubble-transport "Learn more about bubble transport from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), membrane optimization, etc., must be addressed right now before being transferred to GW-scale industrial projects that should be commissioned in 2030.

Introduction
------------

Nowadays, [hydrogen production](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/hydrogen-production "Learn more about hydrogen production from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is dominated by [natural gas reforming](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/natural-gas-reforming "Learn more about natural gas reforming from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), which gives rise to gray hydrogen, followed by gasification of coal and [petroleum coke](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/petroleum-coke "Learn more about petroleum coke from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), which are all extremely CO 2 emissive technologies (e.g., around 8–10 kg CO 2 per kg of gray hydrogen produced). In this context, renewable hydrogen (historically referred to as “green hydrogen”) produced by water electrolyzers connected to [renewable power plants](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/renewable-power-plant "Learn more about renewable power plants from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is expected to be a cornerstone in the green energy transition, notably by decarbonizing the “hard-to-abate” sectors (production of ammonia, steel, cement, etc.). It is well admitted that hydrogen can play a key role as a chemical [feedstock](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/feedstock "Learn more about feedstock from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and an energy carrier for drastically reducing carbon emissions in industry, heating, and transport.

In 2021, roughly 500 MW of water electrolyzers[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2542435124000953#bib1) had been set up and produced approximatively 40 kt of hydrogen worldwide, which remained quite negligible compared with a global demand for hydrogen of 90 Mt.[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2542435124000953#bib2)

As of today, China is leading the world in terms of renewable [hydrogen production](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/hydrogen-production "Learn more about hydrogen production from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") activity by having launched the largest industrial projects (590 MW referenced in 2022). Shell has started a 20 MW [electrolyzer](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/electrolyzer "Learn more about electrolyzer from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in Zhangjiakou, Hebei Province, supplying [fuel cell vehicles](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/fuel-cell-vehicle "Learn more about fuel cell vehicles from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") for the 2022 Winter Olympic Games. Ningxia Baofeng Energy Group has completed the world’s largest electrolytic hydrogen project on December 2022, with a 150 MW electrolyzer and a 200 MW solar array coupled with the grid, in Ningxia, central China.[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2542435124000953#bib3) Finally, the Chinese oil company Sinopec has built an even bigger electrolysis plant in inner Mongolia, with a 260 MW electrolyzer powered by a 450 MW wind and 270 MW solar capacity plant, for an expected annual production capacity of 30 kt of renewable hydrogen.[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2542435124000953#bib4) The latest news actually shows that the operation of such a plant is far from trivial.[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2542435124000953#bib5)

Overall, renewable hydrogen demand is expected to drastically grow within the next years and decades. For instance, the European Commission recently announced in its REPowerEU plan an expected domestic production of 10 Mt of renewable hydrogen in Europe by 2030, and to import the same quantity from abroad.[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2542435124000953#bib6)

At the global scale, the hydrogen demand estimates for 2030 are quite scattered, with figures going from 21.4 Mt (IRENA)[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2542435124000953#bib7) to 90 Mt to answer the net zero emission (NZE) scenario of IEA.[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2542435124000953#bib8) On the other hand, estimates converge more for 2050. A comparison of the different estimation reports (IEA, IRENA, BNEF, Hydrogen Council) given in the IRENA’s report on “Geopolitics of the Energy Transformation: The Hydrogen Factor” gives a range between 400 and 550 Mt for the worldwide demand of low carbon hydrogen in 2050, with ∼2/3 of this hydrogen being produced through electrolysis (while the remaining 1/3 would be blue hydrogen, which is gray hydrogen coupled with CO 2 capture and storage).[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2542435124000953#bib9)

Beyond the enormous effort required in terms of low carbon electricity deployment to be able to answer the power supply challenge, there are still some concerns to overcome for developing very large-scale [water electrolysis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/water-electrolysis "Learn more about water electrolysis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") plants to massively [produce hydrogen](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/produce-hydrogen "Learn more about produce hydrogen from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), notably the lack of data about the behavior of electrolyzers with power intermittency, the safety of large-scale installations, the availability of raw materials, or even the environmental impact of their fabrication and installation. Therefore, the academic community and industries must focus their research efforts on some key fundamental and applied scientific topics to unlock the potential of electrolytic H 2 as a decarbonization solution.

The place of alkaline electrolysis in the industrial renewable H 2 production
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------

### GW-scale perspective: Proton exchange membrane vs. alkaline electrolyzers

Nowadays, there are two major technologies [of water electrolysis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/electrolysis-of-water "Learn more about of water electrolysis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), namely the alkaline (ALK) electrolysis and the [polymer electrolyte](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/polymer-electrolyte "Learn more about polymer electrolyte from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") membrane (PEM) electrolysis, that tend to be largely deployed industrially, being gradually optimized toward pressurized systems. Alkaline electrolysis is the most mature, being used for more than a century. Indeed, large-scale [alkaline electrolysis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/alkaline-electrolysis "Learn more about alkaline electrolysis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") plants, rated at 100 MW, were already developed during the first half of the 20th century to be able to answer the huge demand for hydrogen in the production of ammonia fertilizers and explosives. During all this period, the electrolysis plants were powered by hydroelectricity, mainly in [mountainous regions](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/mountainous-region "Learn more about mountainous regions from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Then, due to the exploitation of hydrocarbons, the production of cheaper hydrogen has been made possible via processes like [steam methane reforming](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/methane-steam-reforming "Learn more about steam methane reforming from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (usually referred to as gray H 2), [partial oxidation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/partial-oxidation "Learn more about partial oxidation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of oil in addition to [coal gasification](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/coal-gasification "Learn more about coal gasification from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Hence, water [electrolysis technology](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/electrolysis-technology "Learn more about electrolysis technology from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") was not much developed in the last decades, compared with other electrolysis processes such as chlorine or [aluminum](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/aluminum "Learn more about aluminum from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") production.

It was the oil crisis period coupled with a strong effort in space research that promoted the emergence of the [PEM technology](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/membrane-technology "Learn more about PEM technology from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in the 1970s. PEM electrolysis is attractive, notably in terms of dynamic behavior, current densities, and compactness, but [PEM cells](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/cell-membrane "Learn more about PEM cells from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") utilize [iridium](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/iridium "Learn more about iridium from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[oxide](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/oxide-compound "Learn more about oxide from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") catalysts at the [positive electrode](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/positive-electrode "Learn more about positive electrode from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Amounts of iridium are very limited worldwide, the annual production rate is between 7 to 9 t/year.[10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2542435124000953#bib10) Therefore, if no significant progress is made in iridium content reduction, a 1 GW-scale plant would require between 5% and 10% of the worldwide production. Recent studies pointed out that the iridium demand for a mature PEM water [electrolyzer](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/electrolyzer "Learn more about electrolyzer from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (PEMWE) market cannot be covered from mine production with current production rates, and thus constitutes a real bottleneck.[10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2542435124000953#bib10) This is due to the scarcity of the element, its geographical concentration in South Africa, and coupling of its production rate to the primary [platinum group metals](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/platinum-group-element "Learn more about platinum group metals from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (PGMs), e.g., [palladium](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/palladium "Learn more about palladium from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [rhodium](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/rhodium "Learn more about rhodium from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and [ruthenium](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/ruthenium "Learn more about ruthenium from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Another widely identified concern is related to the use of Nafion™ as based material for the membrane. Indeed, [Nafion](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/nafion "Learn more about Nafion from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") belongs to PFAS materials (per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances), and these chemicals are pointed as forever pollutants due to their chemical stability and could be banned in the EU in the short term because of their potential toxicity.[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2542435124000953#bib11) Although crucial, this issue must not cast a shadow over the massive use of PFAS in coatings and/or gaskets or insulating tapes used in both PEM and alkaline electrolyzers.[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2542435124000953#bib12)

Although being assumed to be “mature,” these two technologies need to improve significantly, especially regarding efficiency, current density, and capital expense (CAPEX) reduction. Interestingly, [Figure 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2542435124000953#fig1) highlights the discrepancies between performance and costs targets fixed by the EU and the Department of Energy (DOE) in the US. Notably, the CAPEX targeted by the US DOE to reach H 2 production at 1$/kg by 2030[13](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2542435124000953#bib13) is 10 times lower than the one forecasted by the EU[14](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2542435124000953#bib14) ([Figures 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2542435124000953#fig1)C and 1D). These discrepancies highlight the different methodologies used in these reports: although DOE sets targets required to reach their cost objective, the EU tries rather to predict the manufacturing cost of electrolyzers based on market deployment and scale-up cost reductions. In both cases, achieving this very low CAPEX is very challenging and would surely require investigating novel electrolyzer designs to reduce the [materials footprint](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/material-footprint "Learn more about materials footprint from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") used (steel, etc.) while improving their energy efficiency.

### Ongoing and future projects: From few MW to uncertain GW

#### GW-scale deployment prerequisite

Alkaline electrolyzer electrodes are historically based on non-noble metals, typically Ni or [Ni alloys](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/ni-alloy "Learn more about Ni alloys from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), which are much more available. In this context, one can easily conclude that the GW-scale deployment of renewable H 2 plant will be at least partly based upon alkaline electrolyzers.

However, till today, only few water electrolyzers projects have been sanctioned and have reached the final investment decision (FID), i.e., being in P4, as shown in [Figure 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2542435124000953#fig2)D. This is partly due to interrogations regarding the demand of renewable hydrogen, the actual cost of production, and the real maturity of the [electrolyzer technologies](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/electrolyzer-technology "Learn more about electrolyzer technologies from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") for these GW-scale projects.

To start with, the cost of production of renewable H 2 needs to be competitive with the classical fossil-based hydrogen (∼2$/kg). Even considering an electricity price such as $20/MWh, and a very good efficiency of 50 kWh/kg, changing the stacks only twice during the plant operation and deliberately ignoring other costs such as water/utilities, manpower, etc. the CAPEX required to produce H 2 below $2/kg would be as low as 400 $/kW (total installed CAPEX) ([Figure 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2542435124000953#fig3)). As of today, alkaline electrolyzers are beyond these targets. Moreover, the most advanced alkaline electrolyzers are using [cathodes and anodes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/anodes-and-cathode "Learn more about cathodes and anodes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") based on PGM or other scarce [metal coatings](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/metal-coating "Learn more about metal coatings from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), which can limit the price reduction. The ramping up of manufacturing volumes might not be enough and cannot be the sole lever to reduce drastically the costs. As a consequence, substantial R&D efforts need to be launched to reduce the stack cost, increase the efficiency, and eventually reach this ambitious goal.

The apparent high level of maturity of alkaline electrolysis gives the illusion that the technology is fully understood and mastered, which can lead to a risk of loss of interest from academics who turn to less mature technologies where the time to market is much longer.

Alkaline electrolyzers hitherto developed were connected to hydroelectricity, which meant a stable source of electricity. For future projects based on fluctuating renewable electricity sources, alkaline electrolyzers will require accommodating power load intermittency while preserving high efficiency and [safety standards](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/safety-standard "Learn more about safety standards from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Operational and safety issues recently pointed out in the Sinopec project related to the fluctuating nature of the input power clearly demonstrate the challenges industry will have to face ahead.

From a development perspective, it is key to notice that these GW-scale projects will take some time before being commissioned ([Figure 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2542435124000953#fig2)C). Basically, such projects will be achieved by phases until the full commissioning. As an example, for a single phase of a project, a preliminary study, which is conducted even before requesting information from potential suppliers, might take about 1 year (P1). Then, conceptual and pre-project studies will lead to the selection of the electrolyzer supplier, which might take 18 months to 2 years (P2). Finally, the FID is taken 1 year later, after the front-end engineering design (FEED) phase (P3), and the first electrolyzer will finally arrive on the plant roughly 1 year later (P4). This means that electrolyzers used in projects currently investigated will be onstream in about 5 years. ## Safety and efficiency problems of hydrogen production from alkaline water electrolyzers driven by renewable energy sources

#### Abstract

Alkaline water electrolysis has become the most promising solution for hydrogen production considering certain factors such as cost, lifetime and maturity of technology. By developing the large-scale hydrogen production by alkaline water electrolysis as the energy buffering, the consuming pressure of the power system caused by the growing penetration of renewable energy sources such as wind and solar energy can be relieved effectively. However, when the alkaline water electrolysis is integrated with renewable energy sources, the unclear underlying mechanism and interaction characteristics limit large-scale application for hydrogen production. Aiming at this problem, from the aspects of efficiency and consistency, power regulation flexibility and gas purity, this paper studies safety and efficiency problems about the wide-range operation of alkaline water electrolyzers driven by renewable energy sources. The corresponding mechanism analyses are carried out and possible solutions for the future research are given, which are expected to provide a comprehensive review and useful guide for this research topic.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Alkaline water electrolysis (AWE) is a mature technology with over a century of development, demonstrating reliability in hydrogen production for various industrial applications.
AWEs utilize abundant materials and simpler designs, making them cost-effective for large-scale hydrogen production, especially when powered by renewable energy sources.
Despite its advantages, AWE faces efficiency challenges, particularly at low loads, where performance drops and operational inconsistencies can arise due to fluctuating power supplies.
AWE technology has a significant market presence, with numerous ongoing projects globally, reflecting increasing demand for renewable hydrogen and the transition towards sustainable energy solutions.
